Name: adaptive_quantization_scaling
Title: Adaptive Quantization Scaling: A Mechanistic Approach to Latency-Aware Mixed-Precision Inference
Short Hypothesis: Leveraging a mechanistic understanding of layer sensitivity, we can develop an adaptive quantization scaling framework that dynamically adjusts the precision of individual layers during inference, achieving a more efficient balance between latency and accuracy without the need for extensive training.
Related Work: Existing works such as the Adaptive Mixed Precision Quantization (AMP) and FlexiQ primarily focus on static mixed-precision settings or require extensive training to optimize quantization. In contrast, our proposal introduces a real-time adaptive mechanism based on layer sensitivity to optimize quantization on-the-fly, reducing the overhead typically associated with searching for optimal configurations.
Abstract: In this proposal, we introduce Adaptive Quantization Scaling (AQS), a novel framework designed to optimize the inference performance of deep neural networks through dynamic layer-wise mixed-precision quantization. AQS leverages a mechanistic understanding of layer sensitivities to adaptively adjust the bit-width of each layer during inference based on real-time input characteristics and latency constraints. By employing a Jacobian-based layer-skipping strategy, AQS minimizes hidden-state perturbations while adhering to strict wall-clock budgets. Our experiments will demonstrate that AQS achieves superior latency-accuracy trade-offs compared to static quantization methods, enabling efficient deployment of deep learning models on resource-constrained hardware. We will evaluate AQS using a suite of benchmarks, focusing on throughput metrics and stability radii to quantify performance improvements over existing adaptive quantization frameworks.
Experiments: 1. Implement AQS with a focus on dynamic layer sensitivity assessment through perturbation analysis and evaluate the accuracy-latency trade-offs on standard datasets such as ImageNet and CIFAR-10.
2. Conduct a series of latency benchmarks comparing AQS against static mixed-precision quantization methods to assess improvements in throughput and latency under varying load conditions.
3. Analyze the stability of performance by measuring the variance in accuracy and latency across multiple runs to ensure robustness under different input distributions.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Potential risks include the accuracy loss in dynamic adaptation if layer sensitivity is misestimated. Additionally, real-time adjustments may introduce computational overhead that could negate latency benefits in extreme cases. The framework’s performance may also depend on the underlying hardware compatibility for efficient execution.
